---
title: Build on OpenBody
description: Start your app with a proven health & fitness data model, a CC0 exercise registry, and free import from ten formats — portability as a launch feature.
---

import { Aside, Card, CardGrid, LinkCard } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';
import meta from '../../../generated/spec-meta.json';

If you're building a fitness, training, or health app, one of your day-one jobs is
inventing a data model: how a workout, a set, a superset, a planned session, an interval,
a heart-rate stream are shaped. That work is expensive to get right, invisible to your
users, and worth nothing competitively — and a hand-rolled v1 schema usually turns into a
painful migration two years in.

You can skip it. OpenBody is an open, vendor-neutral data model you can adopt **natively**
— not just as an export format at the boundary, but as the shape of your domain.

## What you get on day one

<CardGrid>
  <Card title="A curated, CC0 exercise database" icon="list-format">
    Every fitness app needs an exercise database. The
    [OpenBody registry](/registry/exercises/) ships **800+ canonical movements** with
    structured facets (equipment, movement pattern, anatomy, laterality), progression /
    regression / variation relationships, and crosswalks from the names real apps use —
    CC0, no attribution required, versioned, and growing. This alone is worth adopting;
    the format rides in with it.
  </Card>
  <Card title="A data model that's already solved the hard parts" icon="seti:json">
    Supersets and drop sets, planned-versus-performed, %1RM and threshold-relative
    targets, AMRAP/EMOM scoring, continuous efforts, per-side work, sport outcomes,
    provenance and correction lifecycles — modeled once, [specified precisely](/specification/),
    stress-tested across everything from powerlifting to freediving to kabaddi. You
    inherit answers, not open questions.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Free import from ten formats" icon="random">
    The Apache-2.0 [reference library](https://github.com/openbody/openbody-ts) maps
    Hevy, Strong, Strava, Apple Health, Garmin FIT (from [decoded messages](/mapping/fit/)),
    GPX, TCX, Fitbit, Concept2, and theCrag exports into OpenBody — see the full
    [supported-formats matrix](/mapping/) — resolves exercise names to canonical ids,
    and exports back out (Strong-format CSV, which Hevy also imports). "Bring your
    history from any app" becomes a launch feature you didn't have to build.
  </Card>
  <Card title="Portability as a trust signal" icon="approve-check">
    Platforms are tightening data access, and the EU Data Act is pushing the other way.
    "Your data is portable, in an open format" is a real differentiator against
    incumbents whose lock-in users increasingly resent — and it costs you nothing when
    the format is your native shape.
  </Card>
</CardGrid>

## Two ways to adopt

**OpenBody-shaped domain model.** Keep whatever storage suits your queries (Postgres,
SQLite, Core Data) but make your domain types mirror the OpenBody model — `Session`,
`Block`, `WorkUnit`, `Target`, `ExerciseRef` — and speak OpenBody JSON at every boundary:
import, export, sync, and API. You get the modeling and interop wins without committing
your storage layer.

**OpenBody documents as storage.** For local-first and smaller apps, storing OpenBody
records directly (JSON documents in SQLite, files, or a sync store) is genuinely viable:
records are self-contained, immutable-plus-supersession by design (a natural fit for
sync), and your "database export" is *already* the interchange format. The
[conformance vectors](/conformance/) double as your test fixtures.

Either way, the rules that protect you as a consumer are simple: read what you understand,
**preserve what you don't** (unknown fields and tokens round-trip; nothing is dropped),
and use [namespaced extensions](/specification/) for anything app-specific — your private
fields never collide with the core or with other apps.

## What we promise about stability

- The spec follows **semantic versioning** with a published
  [versioning policy](https://github.com/openbody/openbody/blob/main/VERSIONING.md):
  additive changes are minors, breaking changes only at majors, and elements are
  **deprecated, not removed**, within a major line.
- Open vocabularies grow via **registry releases, not spec releases** — new exercises,
  disciplines, or scoring schemes never require a core version bump, and unknown tokens
  always round-trip.
- Conformance is judged by **published test vectors** with the reference implementation
  as the oracle — you don't have to implement canonicalization machinery to be
  conformant; your inputs and outputs are what count.

<Aside type="caution" title="Where we are today (honest status)">
OpenBody is a **pre-v1.0 draft (spec v{meta.specVersion})** in early access — the repos are
public, but breaking changes are still possible before v1.0, and
`@openbody/openbody-ts` is not yet on npm. If you're evaluating OpenBody as your native
format **now**, you are exactly the adopter we most want to hear from: early integrators
get direct input into what stabilizes and how — the practical path is a short
integration conversation, not a silent dependency. Reach us at
<a href="mailto:contact@openbody.dev">contact@openbody.dev</a>.
</Aside>
